policy_statement: Armenia aims to reduce electricity distribution losses to 6.4% in 2027 under Electric Networks of Armenia's 2016-2027 investment program.
mechanism_description: An investment program to reduce electricity distribution losses lowers the amount of electricity that must be generated to deliver the same end-use consumption, reducing associated power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same electricity service delivered → ↓ transmission/distribution losses → ↓ electricity generation set → ↓ power-sector GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/grid operators upgrade and operate distribution networks to cut technical losses (e.g., conductor upgrades, transformer replacement, voltage optimization) and/or non-technical losses (e.g., metering, theft reduction).  
2) Mediating physical process: Lower resistive (I²R) and equipment losses and improved accounting reduce energy dissipated as heat or unbilled consumption on the network, decreasing total energy that must be produced to meet delivered demand.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced generation (especially from fossil units on the margin and/or reduced overall fossil share) leads to lower CO₂e emissions from the electricity supply chain.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided electricity generation due to lower grid losses, which reduces power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Lower distribution losses reduce required electricity generation for the same delivered consumption, yielding direct GHG reductions from the power sector.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While loss reduction can improve reliability and resilience (an adaptation co-benefit) and can be framed as efficiency, the core climate-relevant endpoint is reduced GHG emissions from avoided generation, which is mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a grid is near-fully renewable, loss reduction yields smaller direct GHG benefits (more an efficiency/reliability outcome), but the mechanism as defined still targets avoided generation and thus emissions reductions where fossil generation exists or on the margin.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces distribution losses necessarily reduces required upstream generation to deliver the same end-use electricity, so the primary mitigation pathway holds across contexts (magnitude varies by grid emissions intensity).  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype (e.g., “grid efficiency,” “utility modernization,” “loss reduction/technical vs non-technical”) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific measures and grid mix evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In very low-carbon grids, the dominant climate effect may shift toward resource efficiency/reliability rather than large GHG reductions; also, if demand rebounds due to lower losses/costs, net emissions benefits can be partially offset.
key_indicators: “Reduction of distribution losses to 6.4% in 2027”; “ENA 2016-2027 Investment program”
co_benefits: Improved grid reliability/quality of supply; reduced operating costs; potential reduction in local air pollutants via lower fossil generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an infrastructure investment program, but the text provided is primarily a quantified loss-reduction target rather than specifying particular capital works.
